title: SBTI Personality Test
description: Use 50 questions to map your social drive, boldness, thinking structure, and independent momentum. Results are generated on submit and saved locally.
disclaimer: This is a lightweight self-reflection tool, not a clinical assessment. It is best used to understand how you work, communicate, and move decisions forward.
intro: Go with the answer that feels most natural, not the answer that sounds ideal. Results stay hidden until you complete and submit the full test.
historyDescription: Submitted results are automatically saved in localStorage so you can revisit them later.
emptyHistory: No saved result yet. Complete all 50 questions and submit to create one.
incompleteDescription: The test is not ready to submit yet. Complete every question for a full result.
readyDescription: All questions are complete. Submit now to generate your profile and save it to local history.
savedDescription: This result has been written to browser local storage, so you can reopen it later.
assessmentHeadline: Your profile appears after all 50 answers
assessmentDescription: We keep the result hidden until the end so the earlier scores do not bias how you answer the remaining questions.

s.name: Social drive
s.description: How much energy you gain from interaction, expression, and connection.

b.name: Bold action
b.description: Whether you naturally move first or hold for more certainty.

t.name: Thinking structure
t.description: How strongly you rely on logic, frameworks, and evidence.

i.name: Independent drive
i.description: How well you self-start and hold momentum without outside structure.

questions.prompt: In unfamiliar settings, I am usually willing to start the conversation first.
questions.hint: Think first meetings, workshops, or new collaborations.

questions.prompt: In group discussions, I tend to voice ideas early instead of polishing them privately for a long time.
questions.hint: Notice your natural speaking rhythm.

questions.prompt: If I spend too much time alone, my energy noticeably drops.
questions.hint: Do interactions help you recharge?

questions.prompt: After meetings, I often remember shifts in attitude and tone, not just the outcome.
questions.hint: How strongly do you track emotional and relational signals?

questions.prompt: I like to feel people out through conversation before jumping straight into tasks.
questions.hint: Do you naturally create connection before execution?

questions.prompt: When a room feels quiet or awkward, I naturally want to warm it up.
questions.hint: For example by asking questions, bridging topics, or adding energy.

questions.prompt: When conflict happens, I care first about whether the relationship still feels okay.
questions.hint: Do you protect connection before pushing the point?

questions.prompt: When I need long stretches of solo work, I usually feel more comfortable than I do in constant collaboration.
questions.hint: A higher score means solo time feels easier for you.

questions.prompt: I am happy to bring unfinished ideas into conversation instead of waiting for them to be complete.
questions.hint: Do you use conversation to shape your thinking?

questions.prompt: After dense social interaction, I usually need a long time to feel quiet again.
questions.hint: A higher score means social energy drains you faster.

questions.prompt: With strangers, I can usually find common ground fairly quickly.
questions.hint: Notice how fast you build a bridge.

questions.prompt: I prefer thinking out loud with people more than thinking everything through before I speak.
questions.hint: Is expression itself part of your thinking process?

questions.prompt: If a whole day passes with very little human interaction, I usually do not mind much.
questions.hint: A higher score means you need less social stimulation.

questions.prompt: When a task is still fuzzy, I usually make a first pass and learn from that.
questions.hint: Do you move through uncertainty by testing?

questions.prompt: Under time pressure, I would rather ship an 80% version and improve it than wait for perfect confidence.
questions.hint: Notice your tolerance for iteration.

questions.prompt: I often wait to see how others approach something before deciding whether to act.
questions.hint: A higher score means you prefer to observe first.

questions.prompt: When there is no clear precedent, I am still comfortable being the first to try.
questions.hint: Especially when others are unsure.

questions.prompt: I can accept acting first and correcting later instead of needing certainty from the start.
questions.hint: How much do you trust calibration through motion?

questions.prompt: If the downside is manageable, I usually start fairly quickly.
questions.hint: What counts as 'safe enough' for you?

questions.prompt: Even without a clear process, I will often push things one step forward.
questions.hint: Do you create momentum for yourself?

questions.prompt: I often delay starting because I am worried about making mistakes.
questions.hint: A higher score means caution slows you down more often.

questions.prompt: Once the direction is mostly clear, I do not need every detail before moving.
questions.hint: How much missing information can you tolerate?

questions.prompt: I would rather learn from fast feedback than spend a long time predicting everything upfront.
questions.hint: Do you trust short loops more than long forecasts?

questions.prompt: Once I decide to move, I can usually enter execution mode very quickly.
questions.hint: Is there much lag between decision and motion?

questions.prompt: With new tools or methods, I usually want to try them directly instead of watching first.
questions.hint: Notice whether novelty pulls you into exploration or caution.

questions.prompt: Before important work, I want every likely risk rehearsed before I begin.
questions.hint: A higher score means you lean heavily toward preparation before action.

questions.prompt: Before deciding, I usually break the problem into smaller judgment points.
questions.hint: Criteria, variables, trade-offs, and priorities.

questions.prompt: With complex information, I prefer to build a framework first and then add details.
questions.hint: Do you naturally create structure?

questions.prompt: In discussions, I care whether everyone is using the same definitions.
questions.hint: Do you naturally align boundaries and meaning?

questions.prompt: If an idea sounds right but the logic chain feels incomplete, I do not fully trust it.
questions.hint: How much do you need a clean reasoning loop?

questions.prompt: I like turning experience into reusable methods instead of only remembering the outcome.
questions.hint: Do you naturally build models from examples?

questions.prompt: When people give advice, I first want to know the basis behind it.
questions.hint: Evidence, pattern, experience, or guess?

questions.prompt: I often judge by feel and do not really want to map the logic in detail.
questions.hint: A higher score means intuition leads more than structure.

questions.prompt: When tackling work, I prefer to define the success standard before deciding how to start.
questions.hint: What counts as done, good, urgent, or sufficient?

questions.prompt: I naturally separate facts, guesses, and conclusions.
questions.hint: Do you mentally layer information?

questions.prompt: In retrospectives, I look for system causes more than emotional snapshots.
questions.hint: Do you place issues back into structure?

questions.prompt: When there is too much information, I tend to sketch a map, list, or structure for myself.
questions.hint: Do you need visible organization to think clearly?

questions.prompt: I think 'close enough' is usually more useful than trying to define things precisely.
questions.hint: A higher score means precision matters less to you.

questions.prompt: Even without reminders, I can keep a plan moving forward.
questions.hint: Think about unsupervised work.

questions.prompt: On important choices, I prefer to form my own first draft of judgment before inviting outside opinions.
questions.hint: Not ignoring feedback, just building your own base first.

questions.prompt: When nobody assigns the task, I will still set my own pace and next step.
questions.hint: Can you create structure out of blank space?

questions.prompt: In long projects, I usually do not need much external supervision.
questions.hint: Can internal momentum carry you?

questions.prompt: If feedback is delayed, I can still keep working instead of stalling.
questions.hint: How dependent are you on outside response?

questions.prompt: On key decisions, I can rely too heavily on other people's confirmation.
questions.hint: A higher score means outside validation matters more.

questions.prompt: I am better at exploring a path myself first and then bringing others in.
questions.hint: Do you naturally build the first route on your own?

questions.prompt: When the rules are unclear, I usually wait for someone else to define the direction first.
questions.hint: A higher score means you lean more on external framing.

questions.prompt: I can prioritize work for myself instead of always waiting for outside ranking.
questions.hint: Do you have a stable inner sorting system?

questions.prompt: When working independently, I usually enter focus more easily.
questions.hint: Notice how your attention behaves in solo mode.

questions.prompt: Even if the team rhythm is messy, I can usually protect my own momentum.
questions.hint: How much does outside disorder shake your progress?

questions.prompt: When I hit resistance, I usually need other people to keep pushing me so I stay in motion.
questions.hint: A higher score means you depend more on external drive.

s.title: Atmosphere Connector
s.description: You naturally shape momentum through conversation, tone, and human connection. You are often the person who turns a room into a working team.
strengths: Starts conversations and builds trust quickly
Reads shifts in group energy well
Strong fit for alignment, coordination, and relationship-heavy roles
s.caution: Make sure speed of response does not replace depth of thought. Leave yourself enough room to process before committing.
s.environment: Great fit for kickoffs, cross-functional syncs, community work, client conversations, and collaborative launches.

b.title: Momentum Driver
b.description: You carry natural forward motion. When conditions are messy, you are still able to create the next step and get things unstuck.
strengths: Strong fit for pilots, launches, and fast-moving execution work
b.caution: Keep some space for others to process the direction, so the team is not only following pace but actually aligned.
b.environment: Best in experiments, growth work, high-pressure sprints, new initiatives, and recovery situations.

t.title: Systems Thinker
t.description: You naturally look for structure, evidence, and patterns. You are often strongest when a fuzzy situation needs a clear model.
strengths: Builds frameworks and decision logic well
Strong fit for diagnosis, planning, design, and strategic review
t.caution: Do not let the desire for a perfect model delay momentum. Small experiments can sharpen your thinking, not weaken it.
t.environment: Best in planning, retrospectives, system design, product reasoning, and ambiguous problem-solving.

i.title: Independent Builder
i.description: You trust your own rhythm and can carry motion without much outside pressure. Self-directed progress is one of your core strengths.
strengths: Builds personal paths in ambiguous situations
Strong fit for research, solo ownership, and early exploration
i.caution: Independent does not mean isolated. Add intentional checkpoints so your path stays sharp and does not narrow too early.
i.environment: Best in exploration, long-form work, independent projects, and contexts where you must set the rhythm yourself.

blends.sb: You can energize people and move them into action. Strong fit for early-stage leadership and kickoff moments.
blends.st: You translate complex thinking into human language. Strong fit for alignment, teaching, and shared understanding.
blends.si: You build trust and hold steady motion. Strong fit for long-term relationship work and supportive leadership.
blends.bs: You tend to pull teams out of hesitation and into movement. Strong fit for execution under pressure.
blends.bt: You balance action with judgment, which makes you strong in high-stakes execution and decision-heavy work.
blends.bi: You have a natural starter engine. Strong fit for building from zero or taking ownership quickly.
blends.ts: You bring structure into communication. Strong fit for explanation, planning reviews, and converting logic into alignment.
blends.tb: You design the frame and then push it into reality. Strong fit for steering direction and execution together.
blends.ti: You are a classic quiet strategist. Strong fit for depth, design, and independent complex problem-solving.
blends.is: You combine self-direction with relational steadiness. Strong fit for calm leadership and guided ownership.
blends.ib: You bring both self-starting energy and forward momentum. Strong fit for high-autonomy, high-accountability work.
blends.it: You solve deeply and steadily. Strong fit for thoughtful execution where clarity matters more than noise.
